reason: Verified against files; the change is concretely sound and I am not uncertain, so refuted=false. (1) No defect class is under-flagged: the new clause carves out ONLY bounded full-row hydration (reads capped by MAX_PAGE_SIZE), and explicitly preserves high for unbounded scans / N+1 / pool exhaustion / super-linear-on-unbounded-input. The bad fixture's two genuine defects -- select(Item.id) with NO .limit (unbounded full-table scan) and session.get(Item, iid) in a loop (N+1) -- are exactly the classes the clause keeps at high, and are untouched by the fixture edits (good-fixture-only). Reviewers rated them high 3/3 across r0-r2, including pool-exhaustion. (2) No known-bad slips: block_threshold stays high; the bad fixture is unbounded and NOT routed through list_items, so the bounded-read carve-out does not reach it. (3) The good fixture is genuinely clean by the codebase bar -- VERIFIED: template repository.list_items does select(Item) (hydrates id,name,created_at) and routes/items.py reuses it; the good fixture's get_item_names reuses list_items and discards columns, the identical idiom the template ships unflagged. The baseline low over-flag (3/3, line 46) penalizes the new code for a pattern the surrounding codebase already does, which is precisely the codebase-bar/internal-consistency over-flag the rubric+clause suppress. The fixture edit makes that good case unambiguously clean (target []), it does not make it dirty. (4) No bad case is made ambiguous: the distinguishing signal (unbounded/N+1 vs bounded list_items reuse) is robust and the reviewers already separate them correctly. Every factual claim in the changelist entry checks out against src/framework_cli/review/agents/performance.md, the fixtures, the template, and the baseline findings. Priority P2 / NEEDS_TUNING is accurate.
